summary: Kwami-Ai appears to be a React/Vite/Redux TypeScript web app (chat, services, checklist, profile pages with an AI API layer) — a plausible hackathon scope. Commit timing is clean (all 14 commits inside the window, none before T0, ~22 min median cadence), but a single 434K-LOC / 644-file initial commit and a README that is verbatim the redux-thunk library's README raise authenticity questions worth a closer look.
observations: All 14 commits fall within the event window (0 before T0, 0 after T1) with a healthy ~22 min median spacing and a natural spread across 0-12h.
One commit adds 434,124 of the 450,147 total LOC across 644 files — a massive bulk dump that dominates the repo and likely includes package-lock.json and/or committed dependencies.
The application source itself (api/, components/, pages/, store/ with slices) is a coherent, appropriately-sized React+Redux app consistent with AI-assisted hackathon work.
README content is the upstream redux-thunk package README, not a description of this project — suggests boilerplate reuse or an unedited template.
Merge commits present, indicating some real branch/collaboration workflow rather than a single scripted push.
No pre-T0 commits, so no evidence of the codebase being written before the event started.
red_flags: Huge 434K-LOC single initial commit (644 files) could mask pre-built or vendored code and makes the true hand-written scope hard to verify.
README is a copied third-party library README (redux-thunk), not project-specific documentation, hinting at template/boilerplate origins.
